speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: They put on her lice, living lice, and how much they can produce [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to suck her blood. And I was sitting with each hair, taking between my two fingers, and pulling down the dead-- the dead lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was so stupid to ask her, how on earth did you let yourself-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that you didn't care? Then she explained that how she got that. You see, all those things I never knew. I never heard from nobody that what they did [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like with my cousin, who was liberated from Eng-- English soldiers. They put him in cold barrel and in hot barrel. And he got colitis. He almost died. They cut here out pieces for him, constantly. He was very sick after that. You hear those stories what people went through. You can't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with Hannah and you were helping her with the lice. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stole food from those people and tried to feed her, spoon by spoon. It didn't want to stay in her stomach. A long time, nothing. But-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it came, the Red Cross, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and ask, who is from Czechoslovakia? So they took us home. Hannah and me went back to Czechoslovakia, to Prague. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I met there an engineer who was working with my father. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] I was living [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with Hannah there in Prague. But they said that they're looking for us here, because they didn't want the Czech people that we should come back from the Sudeten, because that didn't belong to them. They didn't want-- but anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was looking for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I thought for the American people when I got liberated. I told them that I have in New York an uncle, and his name is Brand. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know in Hungarian, it was [NON-ENGLISH]. It must be [INAUDIBLE] or something like that. And if you could tell him that his sisters-- the others are alive. And I said, Claire Schoenfeld and Hannah Schoenfeld. And that's the way they find me in the newspaper. He put an ad in the paper. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] That's the way I came-- we came to this country in 1946, August the 5th. With the first boat [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the war. That was during the war, just cargo. The [INAUDIBLE] went just without passengers. We were the first passengers on it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I must tell you that because, when the waiter came to my table and brought a tray full of food, and he gave me something on the plate, and I waited and I waited. I didn't let him go. I hold his hand. He shouldn't go away with the tray. He put the food so much up because I hold his hand. He shouldn't take the tray away. I couldn't eat all the food. Because in Prague, we had to stay in line a half a day until you got a loaf of bread, because it-- they didn't have food at all. Or for the whole month we got two pounds of meat, one person. It was rationed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I arrived to United States, and I-- my uncle took me and I saw a hot dog place, I stood there and I says, three, please. And he looks at me. Nobody was close to me. I was alone. That was my first thing. Three, please. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in the United States did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- thank you-- in Czechoslovakia, in Prague, and my uncle, they fought-- they find me. They send, every week for the four of us, letters with money in it. For everybody, $5.00. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And packages. But we were so afraid. Cigarettes-- you could sell it for everything. I wouldn't sell even one cigarette in my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just we had money to buy-- and clothes they sent us. And canned goods constantly. Pam. I learned how to make from that Pam--

speaker: interviewee
text: Spam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spam. All kind of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in my uncle's house in-- in [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Long Beach. He lived near New York, in Long Beach. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He's a builder, too. He was a builder too. He doesn't live any more. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother? Whatever happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-- when my father died, he couldn't come from Spain [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said that they will-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Hungarian people wouldn't let him go back there. They were keep him already. It was too late for him. But my parents were visiting him, I don't know which year. Then, my sister-in-law, and they had a son, a little boy. His name was Claude, because my sister-in-law spoke German or French. She didn't speak Hungarian. And it was bad times then, and my father-- not my father. My mother. Because my father passed away already. My mother asked that he-- if they would let him to come with my mother. And he was by us here. And it was very hard for to take him out. A consul from Spain came and took him out like his son. That's the way he was escaping. My nephew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] died. His wife became very sick. She had a heart condition, and she got the-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] how you-- how you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Heart attack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she got the heart attack. And my brother went for the doctor. And by the time he came, she was dead. And my brother didn't want to live after that, because they were a couple who loved each other very, very much. And he didn't want to see nobody. And a year after my sister-in-law's death-- it wasn't quite a year-- he passed away from sadness. He didn't see nobody. He didn't go out even from the house. Just the maids were there. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this is in Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That was in Mexico City. He was living in Mexico City. He was building there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went there lots of time from this country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to tell you, my children went 10 months schooling to three different countries. Not for pleasure. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We started in California, school for the kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And things were very bad for us here. And we went to Mexico City to my brother, and they went in English school there. And then we went to Canada, where my husband's family was, to finish one term of schooling. I don't even know exactly how old they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about what your husband, and when you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my husband through some friends of my parents. When I came to United States, right away they tried to introduce me to acquaintances. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Always that I should meet somebody. I met that- that family was my father's friends and some distant relatives. And they said to me, you come every Thursday to us for dinner. They lived on Riverside Drive in New York. And I went every Thursday there. And she says to me that, in this building, is a friend of mine, and she has a guest from Canada. He comes from a very good family. If your parents would live, they wouldn't have nothing against him, because he's a nice man. I want you to meet him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, OK. So I met my husband by them. And I saw him and he was bull-headed. And I says to my friend, I says, are you out of your mind? I don't want a bull-headed man. I don't even know how old he is. I don't need a bull-headed man. So she says to me, listen, all my life I am in love with Charles [INAUDIBLE], and he's also bull-headed. I says, so go and marry him. I don't want nothing to do with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was a Thursday, and my husband's supposed to go home for the weekend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me home to the Bronx where I lived. And I says, don't take me home because you won't find your way back. I shouldn't worry about him. He took me home. And since then, he started to call constantly. And he says to me-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I said to him, don't waste your time because I'm not interested. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he didn't give up. And then I got the call from his sister-in-law, because the three of them came out. Two brothers and a sister-in-law came out together from Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got the call from his sister-in-law. They live in Niagara Falls. And she invited me to come and see Niagara Falls. And I said, why not? That's one of the worst-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] first thing-- everybody wants to see Niagara Falls. I says, OK. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in an-- whoever wanted to go somewhere and ask me, I was ready to go always. So I went there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I didn't think that I will marry him, that I'm going there. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that's a long story to say. They were living on a farm, my sister-in-law. And they put me in a bed with so many feather pillows and feather beds that I almost choked there. I says, I can't even lay here. Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went out to see the Niagara Falls at night because it's lit up beautifully. I should see it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came home, and the whole house was dark, and I didn't see light at all. And I says, they're going so soon to sleep here? He says, it looks like that they went to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came in, then the house was full with people-- friends and their relatives. And surprise, they started to say. And without even knowing what goes on, my sister-in-law brought one of her china plate, and she broke it, and she says, mazel tov. You are engaged. [LAUGHS] And I said, no, I don't want to get married. They-- they didn't listen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he came to New York all the time I was living in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I had Sharon and Robin after that. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And two grandchildren-- Elliot and Briana.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Los Ang-- I didn't ask your husband's name. What's your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name is-- it was Alexander. I called him Hungarian Sany-- S-A-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like every Jew who doesn't have a trade, he was a farmer home. So the first thing was, when we came-- when he-- we got married, he-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he bought a farm in Canada. When he was in Ellis Island he had money, and that's the way they didn't ship-- took him back, because he was just for a visit here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a chicken farm. After New York, the first time in my life, when I said, I'm marrying a guy who buy-- bought a chicken farm, all my friends and relatives said that you crazy, you've never been on a farm. I says, I don't know what's a chicken farm is.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- we bought brand new chickens, a day old chickens. And we had to inject them that they should be ready to lay eggs. That was actually an egg farm, not a chicken farm. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was my nicest years in this country. Lot of friends came out every Sunday from New York, and relatives, because it was two hour drive. And we had plenty food, and I always liked lots of company. And it was nice there. Then we-- I said, I don't want to raise my kids on a farm. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was a city girl, and I want my children should go to the city too. So anyway, we came then to California. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My life changed here terribly. We went in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a liquor store business because, like I say, he had money. We went in in the liquor store business. But it was a murder. He didn't see his children. Just Saturday, every Saturday. It was a poppa-momma store. And it opened 8:00 and it was until 12:00 open [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my husband came home at night, after 12:00. The kids were sleeping already. And when he left, they were still sleeping. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We left here-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't even know. I know that the second time when I came, I went, like I said, three different countries the children went to school. I went to my brother to Mexico City. And after that, I went to Canada with them, because I thought, he can start something. But it was worse there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, if I have to go back walking from Canada, I will walk back to California. And then I came-- we came back here in 1957. And since then, we live here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was already over, the gold rush. I didn't find a gold rush anymore. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] And I-- we bought that house in '60, and since then, I'm living here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband passed away 1991. We'd been in Israel just before with my other daughter, Robin, and her husband, and the two children-- grandchildren. We'd been there four weeks. And my husband, you know, in Israel, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] your hotel room is bed with breakfast-- big breakfast you have. Usually we used to have breakfast and no lunch. Just breakfast and dinner. And we'd been in Israel, and my husband didn't want to eat nothing for breakfast. Usually, he used to have coffee, and they had delicious rolls and croissants. He didn't want to touch nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, what's the matter with you, Alex? You don't feel good? He says, I feel good, just my back hurt me. He always complained since I know him that his lower back hurts him. But he didn't want to eat. And I saw that he's, little by little, losing weight.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he didn't complain, just of his back. Nothing else. And he enjoyed his grandchildren very much. He showed off in the hotel for everybody. He went to show off that those are my grandchildren. And when we came back, we started to go to the doctor. We made appointments. Something must be wrong with you because you don't want to eat. You are not on a diet. When he used to be on a diet, he wanted to lose a few pounds because he had a stomach. But-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and when we went to the doctors and they find that he has cancer on his lung. And I took him for treatments-- radiation treatments-- every day [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for cancer of the lung. And it cleared up completely, the cancer on his lung. But he didn't feel better at all. Just worse all the time. He was actually just sick, since he knew that he has cancer, 10 days. And it went, the cancer, from his lung to his liver. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Sharon and I were in the hospital with him, and the doctors were telling me, go home, because he-- nothing will happen with him. Because we've tried to give him medicine that he shouldn't have pain. It will take months until something will happen. Go home because you need some rest. We didn't even leave the hospital. We were just sitting outside, and they came to tell us that he's gone. So he was lucky he didn't suffer, thank God, too much.
